data.title: Celsius' Growing Dollar Share At Compelling Valuations - Dip Buying Opportunity
data.description:
I previously covered Celsius Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ:CELH) in December 2025, discussing why the stock was an even better Buy after the prior market over-reaction to the upcoming inventory transition in FQ4'25.

This was because the Alani Nu acquisition had already triggered the expanding energy drink dollar share and the potentially outsized growth prospects from FQ1'26/FY2026 onwards, aided by the compelling FY2026 valuations and the healthy balance sheet.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am reiterating my Buy rating for the CELH stock, given the stock's notably oversold position during the market-wide pessimism surrounding the uncertain macroeconomic environment and the intensifying market competition.

My optimism is attributed to their expanding energy drink dollar share at the expense of its direct competitors (aided by the Alani Nu and Rockstar acquisitions), the H2'26 volume tailwinds from the ongoing integration into PepsiCo's (PEP) distribution system, and the compelling valuations after the recent correction.

CELH Faces Macro/Competition Headwinds

Since my last Buy rating, the bears have proven my bullish thesis wrong indeed, with CELH already losing -22% of its value against the wider market at -7% and a similar correction also observed in its beverage and Consumer Packaged Goods [CPG] peers in varying degrees.

1. Uncertain Macro & Intensifying Competition

Part of their headwinds are attributed to the increasingly uncertain macroeconomic outlook, with the higher inflationary pressure and the diverging spending trends from the K shaped economy likely to trigger further market pessimism surrounding the CPG sector.

This is especially since the higher fuel prices from the ongoing Iran conflict, currently at $4.01 per gallon as of March 31, 2026 (compared to $2.896 per gallon in December 2025 and $2.61 per gallon in 2019), may potentially trigger reduced discretionary spending trends through the "convenience and gas station retail channels."

This is worsened by the intensifying market competition from private label players, most recently from Costco (COST), attributed to the latter's launch of 24-pack Kirkland Signature Sparkling Energy Drink with three flavor varieties/low calories at an estimated selling price of $0.70 per 12-ounce cans.

When compared to CELH's singular 12-ounce can pricing of $2.28 and bulk 12-ounce can pricing of $1.49 (through the 12-pack), I can understand why the market has decided to de-risk first and ask questions later.

The same has been observed in CELH's stock price correction by -49.3% from the 52 week highs, given the potential shift in consumer buying trends to the cheaper, private label offerings.

2. Dollar Share Gains

Otherwise, I am of the opinion that CELH's selloff has been unwarranted albeit triggering a great dip buying opportunity, attributed to their ability to drive the expanding energy MULO+ w/c dollar share of 19.9% in Q4'25 (+9 points YoY), aided by the Alani Nu and Rockstar acquisitions.

This is especially since their dollar share gains have occurred at the expense of its two direct competitors, Monster Beverage (MNST) at 33.9% in Q4'25 (-0.5 points YoY) and Red Bull at 34.8% (-2.7 points YoY).

Readers must also note that CELH's core offering, CELSIUS® brand, has reported a notable revenue acceleration by +7.5% YoY in FY2025, after the trough +3% YoY observed in FY2024, albeit after the deceleration from the 5Y CAGR of +78.3% (between FY2019 and FY2024, pre Alani Nu and Rockstar acquisitions).

I am of the opinion that there are further growth tailwinds entering FY2026, attributed to the ongoing Alani Nu integration into PEP's distribution system in Q1'26 and Rockstar by Q2'26, with it implying further growth opportunities in H2'26.

The same has been hinted by the management, with the ongoing integration into PEP's distribution system already driving a pro forma Alani Nu revenue growth by +136% YoY in FQ4'25.

Beyond acquisitions, CELH has also highlighted their plans to intensify their product innovation and limited time offer cadence, both of which are likely to "bringing in new guests while increasing the frequency of the existing base" and "maintaining elevated spend and frequency levels throughout the year," similar to Chipotle Mexican Grill's (CMG) renewal strategy.

This reason may also be why CELH has already launched the new Fizz-Free line nationally, with it expected to appeal to new target groups "that prefer beverages without carbonation or like the optionality of fizz or fizz-free."

Combined with the projected multi-year gross margin recovery from FQ4'25 levels of 47.4% to the management target of "low 50s," I am of the opinion that the management remains well positioned to drive a robust, multi-year profitable growth trend ahead.

3. Compellingly Valued, Profitable Growth Trend

Therefore, while CELH has yet to offer any forward guidance, it is unsurprising that the consensus have raised their forward estimates, with the energy drink company expected to report a robust profitable growth from the annualized FQ4'25 levels (attributed to the accretion of Alani Nu from Q2'25 and Rockstar Energy from Q4'25) to the consensus FY2028 estimates.

This is with a top/bottom-line (GAAP EPS) growth estimates at a CAGR of +12.4%/+144.5% through FY2028, with it building upon the historical top-line growth profile at a 5Y CAGR of +80.7% - with it apparent that the recent selloff has been mostly sentiment driven instead of fundamentals.

As a result of the -49.3% meltdown from the 52 week highs and the raised consensus forward estimates (including the accretive impact of Alani Nu and Rockstar Energy acquisitions), I am of the opinion that CELH is rather compelling at FWD EV/EBITDA valuations of 13.86x and FWD P/E GAAP valuations of 22.06x.

Based on the FWD P/E GAAP valuations of 22.06x and the consensus EPS growth estimates at a 3Y CAGR of +110.7%, CELH is even cheaper at my estimated 3Y PEG GAAP ratio of 0.19x, compared to the sector median of 1.07x.

When comparing CELH's FWD EV/EBITDA valuations of 13.86x, FY2025 adj EBITDA performance of 24.6%, and my estimated 3Y PEG GAAP ratio of 0.19x to its peers, including:

  • beverage/snacks - PEP at 12.96x/15.9% in FY2025/1.17x,
  • beverage - Keurig Dr Pepper (KDP) at 8.59x/29.2% in FY2025/0.60x,
  • beverage - MNST at 23.14x/32.4% in FY2025/2.24x, and
  • beverage - Coca-Cola Company (KO) at 20.66x/33% in FY2025/3.67x, respectively,

I am of the opinion that CELH's profitable growth trends are reasonably valued here, significantly aided by the extremely healthy balance sheet at a net debt to adj EBITDA ratio of 0.43x in FY2025 despite the recent Alani Nu acquisition.

This is compared to its direct peers, including PEP at 2.65x, KDP at 0.42x, MNST at 1.02x, and KO at 1.97x.

This development implies that CELH remains well capitalized to drive renewed growth through further product innovation and/or accretive acquisitions, all of which underscoring its compellingly valued, profitable growth investment thesis after the recent selloff.

CELH Remains A Buy For The Contrarian

For now, CELH has already lost much of its 2025/2026 gains, with the stock currently retesting the $33s support levels by the time of writing and it remaining to be seen when the bulls may defend a trading floor.

Otherwise, thanks to the ongoing selloff, there is now an expanded upside potential of +52.6% to my long-term price target of $51.60, based on the FWD P/E GAAP valuations of 22.06x (near to its peer group P/E mean of ~21.85x) and the consensus FY2028 GAAP EPS estimates of $2.34.

Combined with the H2'26 tailwinds from the integration of Alani Nu/Rockstar acquisitions, the multi-year gross margin recovery prospects, and the ongoing cancellation of its dilutive SBC expenses through share repurchases, I am cautiously reiterating my Buy rating for the CELH stock.

This is albeit with the caveat that CELH is only suitable for those with a higher risk appetite and a longer investing trajectory, given:

  • the breach against the November 2025 bottom of $37s,
  • the stock's still elevated short interest ratio of 7.97% despite the ongoing meltdown/oversold technical indicators,
  • the notably pessimistic stock market sentiments, and
  • the lack of forward guidance pending further clarity on their FY2026 growth prospects.

data.title: Nike: Turnaround Advancing As Macro Headwinds Create A Buying Opportunity (Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

Back when I last covered Nike, Inc. (NKE) in November, I titled the article “Progress Amid Tariff Headwinds, But Turnaround Still Needs Time, So I'll Be Patient,” highlighting the company’s internal efforts following the CEO change back in late 2024 and potential to benefit from a macro recovery, while financials remain solid, rating them a Hold since the price was not enough to justify buying it yet.

With the stock now falling to some of its lowest levels in over a decade, Nike is trading below what I’d call a fair value now, with fundamentals still solid and potential to take advantage of a macro recovery while their internal restructuring and turnaround take time, as expected and highlighted before.

The “Win Now” Reality

Despite beating the market’s revenue and EPS estimates, NKE is down following their Q3’FY26 report as a result of the soft guidance, with net income crashing by 35% during Q3 and 32% YTD, as a result of tariff pressure (300 basis points impact on the gross margin), higher effective taxes (20% compared to 5.9% a year ago due to the lack of a one-time non-cash deferred tax benefit), higher selling and administrative expenses, and struggle in their higher-margin DTC segments, on top of their inventory actions.

Regarding the near-term guidance, Nike sees their Q4’FY26 revenue down 2% to 4% as a result of the environment, expecting a 20% drop in China and highlighting the potential disruptions coming from the Iran conflict, targeting to finish their “Win Now” actions by the end of the year. This was significantly worse than the market’s expected 1.9% increase, and it can serve as a wake-up call to the current reality of the business.

As mentioned before, this is not something they can fix overnight (despite the “Win Now” name), as we’re talking about much more than just what the company can do, and I believe the market still has to tame its expectations, at least in the near term, but I’ll detail that in the next segment. The company’s CEO also talked extensively about this during their Q3’FY26 Earnings Call, highlighting:

We're not simply fixing what needs to be fixed. We're building brand by brand, sport by sport, country by country, partner by partner. We're reshaping our marketplace, rewire and how we operate and investing in the technology platforms that we expect will help us serve more consumers better and run our business more effectively. These actions will continue to create near-term pressure, but we believe they are the right actions to strengthen NIKE for the long term and create more durable value for shareholders. This is complex work, and parts of it are taking longer than I'd like, but the direction is clear. The urgency is real, and the foundation is getting stronger. By the end of the calendar year, we expect to have finished our Win Now actions. Aged inventory across the marketplace will be healthy allowing our sports teams to consistently flow athlete-led, innovative and coveted products in all sports across our 3 brands, including NIKE Sportswear and Jordan Streetwear.

Financially, based on Nike’s latest report, we can see yet another advantage, as even though we’re talking about macro weakness and significant internal actions, the company’s current assets are enough to cover their current liabilities and the long-term debt, with the latter dropping by ~$926 million YoY. They also mentioned that the cash position dropped by nearly $2 billion (23%) alongside a ~$400 million (22%) drop in short-term investments, by dividends and buybacks, bond repayments, and CAPEX.

This comes as Nike paid ~$609 million worth of dividends in Q3, 3% higher than a year ago, while the buybacks likely continued to drop (they didn’t mention them), which is a major drop compared to the levels seen in prior years, and that was something I’ve criticized Nike for before, relying on buybacks for EPS growth while the previous CEO didn’t seem to take the right actions and just relied on “Nike being Nike” forever.

I like that the new CEO is willing to take such decisive steps, but this is also something that likely has an effect on the stock, as we’re talking about a company that spent even ~$5.48 billion in buybacks 3 years ago, and I’d rather see them go for buybacks at current levels instead of dividends.

Macro Weakness Is Far From Over

I believe it’s very important to note that we’re not talking about something that’s necessarily a Nike-specific issue, as several other companies—peers and players from completely unrelated industries—have been reporting issues over the past years. This was initially a normal response to the high-rate environment, as they are by design slowing down the economy, hurting borrowing costs and discretionary budgets, but it was then amplified by tariffs (which improved following the Supreme Court’s decisions) and now the Iran conflict.

As such, it's no wonder that Nike and most other apparel companies are reporting weakness in regions such as North America or China (down 12% YTD), although we see some improvements in NA now following their strategy. The CFO also mentioned that they expect Q1’FY27 to be the last one where tariff pressure is material while also explaining their drop in China:

We expect revenues to be down low single digits versus the prior year with gains in North America, offset by declines in Greater China, driven by intentional reduced sell-in and marketplace management actions over that period. While the tariff environment has been uncertain, assuming no significant changes, we expect the first quarter of fiscal '27 to be the final quarter where higher tariffs continue to be a material year-over-year headwind to gross margin. We expect gross margin expansion to begin in the second quarter due to actions to mitigate tariffs and recovery of transitory impacts from Win Now.

However, although we saw some signs of a recovery that could’ve helped Nike’s recovery, that was before the attack against Iran, which also put pressure on the company and its stock throughout March (before the earnings drop). This has the potential to cause significant damage and, at the very least, delay this recovery, as we’re talking about a conflict that has significant implications on the global economy given the region’s importance for oil and gas.

With pump prices already jumping virtually everywhere and many commodity prices (beyond just oil and gas) skyrocketing and now trading at significant war premiums, which can affect discretionary budgets even more, which is crucial when talking about an apparel company like Nike, despite their strong brand power and marketing initiatives, a big reason why a potential turnaround will depend on more than just the company’s abilities.

Although we saw some mixed signals regarding a potential resolution to the conflict and de-escalations—which I believe should be the case given the implications that a prolonged war would have on the global economy, as mentioned in more detail before—the risk of inflationary pressures remains very significant.

This was mentioned by the heads of the Fed and ECB, with the latter even warning that the market may be underestimating the damage done by Iran’s counterattacks to the region’s energy infrastructure, while Qatar’s Energy Minister also mentioned from the early days of the conflict that a recovery to normal flows would still take weeks or even months, which can put more pressure on the consumer and Nike’s valuation, with treasury yields already elevated.

This can further emphasize the K-shaped economic pressure, which can continue to put pressure on Nike’s sales in the near term, although it can also make a recovery more impressive, and it’s important to remember that Nike has the brand power and financials that can help them withstand this weakness far better than other peers.

On the bright side, treasury yields are falling following Trump’s new hints at a potential de-escalation (saying the U.S. might leave Iran “very soon”), although this remains a pending issue and potential escalations are still possible.

Again, Nike’s balance sheet remains solid, and debt pressure wouldn’t be that significant even if the SOFR were to remain elevated (affecting refinancing costs), but these are still very important aspects that have significant implications on the valuation. Lower treasury yields in the longer term and falling borrowing costs can help the company’s valuation and potentially boost the demand for the stock since money would then chase better yields, while discretionary budgets can improve alongside it.

I believe this quote from their CEO emphasizes the reality of the business very well, restating that their recovery will take time and external factors also have a major role, while the market is focusing on near-term pressure:

But what I would tell you is we got our teams reorganized from a product perspective as well in September and spring '27 will be the first time we see the fruits of those teams working together. But in the end, I'm pleased with where we're headed. And I think everybody, when I came into this role, said, "Hey, it's going to take 2 years, and that's where we're tracking right now." So a little bit of both, Brian. We do have some external factors that we're having to deal with while we're in a major comeback, but that's no excuse. We're controlling what we can control. We're getting our teams lined up internally around product and the consumer and storytelling. And then we're getting our country teams lined up around driving a more integrated and elevated marketplace. And ultimately, that's what's going to pay dividends for us and build the foundation for future growth.

Risks Reiterated

Ultimately, Nike’s recovery will depend on more than just the company’s actions, and especially since the CEO warned that a recovery will take time, as mentioned in previous coverage, there’s certainly the possibility of seeing a delay due to the environment.

Meanwhile, competitive pressure remains very elevated given the industry, and Nike’s mistakes can end up costing them significantly, with many competitors ready and able to take advantage of any misstep now, which is a different environment compared to the nearly “blue ocean” they developed in.

Valuation

For Nike’s DCF to Equity valuation, taking into account the significant YTD drop in net income and the restructuring charges, I'll assume Nike can reach an FCF of about $2.5 billion in FY26, which is not far from the TTM levered FCF, without assuming any change in net borrowings since their balance sheet remains strong. Given their initiatives, the FIFA World Cup, potential macro recovery, and normalizing tariffs/reorganization expenses, I’ll assume they can grow this back to $4.5 billion by FY28, which is still below their previous highs and should be in line with the company’s internal actions, even though they expect FY27 to already show the results of their moves.

From this point onwards, given the potential for other macro improvements, buybacks boosting the per share amounts, DTC growth, potential portfolio optimizations (the running business, Converse, etc.), I’ll assume a conservative 6.5% CAGR until FY30 and 5% until FY35, which is below even the market’s revenue estimates, with a 3% terminal growth rate given their pricing/brand power, as well as a 9% discount rate to take into account the macro pressure and demand a better margin of safety, as well as 1.482 billion shares outstanding as of their Q3'FY26 report.

This way, we get to an equity value of $77.39 billion, for an intrinsic value of $52.22 per share, which is still above the current $47.50 at the moment of writing this despite using relatively conservative estimates. As a note, based on these assumptions and the terminal value of $111.38 billion we get to, the ~$6.51 billion FCF seen in FY35 would represent a P/FCF of 17.2, which is very conservative for a company like Nike, and there is certainly potential for much more.

Conclusions

Nike is upgraded to Buy following their drop, with the valuation now offering a solid margin of safety while their turnaround advances, with solid fundamentals and potential to improve alongside the environment.

There are risks to pay attention to given the environment, which can affect Nike in several ways while slowing and limiting their potential turnaround, but the risk-reward is finally attractive following this significant drop, falling to some of their lowest levels in over a decade.

data.title: Don't Trust This Relief Rally, Buy Tech When On Sale
data.description:
America’s military operation in Iran has been ongoing for a little more than a month, and, at the time of writing, markets have just experienced a short relief rally, with the NASDAQ 100-Index (NDX) closing the day up just about 3% yesterday. This followed news that Iran is willing to end the war but “needs guarantees.”

In this article, I present my thoughts about possible long-term energy supply disruptions, how I played the market correction so far, and whether oil at $200+ per barrel is still a likely outcome.

Yes, The Global Economy Is 61% Less “Oil Intense” Than In 1973

Parallels with the 1973 oil crisis are frequent nowadays, with just 100 ships having passed the Strait of Hormuz since the beginning of March. This is fewer ships than what used to pass on average per day before the war.

However, we live in a different world than 1973. Global oil intensity (how much oil is needed to create $1000 of global GDP) has decreased by a whopping 61% since its 1973 peak.

Additionally, consider how Saudi Arabia, the main oil producer in the region, has already found alternatives to the Strait of Hormuz. It has successfully reactivated an East-West pipeline that now pumps 7 million barrels per day. This is equivalent to ~58% of the country’s production capacity of 12 million barrels per day.

The Problem Is That The Impact Of Hormuz Still Surpasses That Of 1973

The disruption of oil supply is only compounded by that of LNG (Liquified Natural Gas), with Qatar being the third-largest exporter of LNG in the world.

As the chart below shows, the impact of oil and LNG disruption in terms of global energy supply far surpasses that of 1973. When accounting for LNG, it stands at 7.5% of global supply, against a disruption of 3.2% in 1973.

And this chart doesn’t account for disruption in other non-energy-related materials. For example, helium, fertilizers, and sulfur. Each of these raw materials has the potential to ignite a crisis independently. At least, it is more likely that a peace deal would ease the exports of these raw materials, while energy supply issues may be lasting.

And Energy Supply Issues May Last Even After A Peace Deal

To understand the magnitude of the situation in Iran, one needs to consider the region’s geography. Virtually all of the region’s oil and LNG is produced in the Persian Gulf or in fields deeper inside Saudi Arabia. At present, any way out of that region passes from a chokepoint:

  • The Strait of Hormuz is the most obvious one and the most direct route out of the Persian Gulf.

  • Exporting via the Red Sea implies passing through the Bab-el-Mandeb Strait, another chokepoint that has historically been attacked by Yemeni rebels, aligned with Iran’s regime.

  • Even the UAE’s ADCOP pipeline, which bypasses the Strait of Hormuz, ends up in Fujairah, which is a port that is well within reach of Iran.

That’s part of the reason why Israel’s Prime Minister has recently floated the idea of a new pipeline that would directly reach the Mediterranean from Saudi Arabia and the UAE via the country itself (see the green dotted line in the map above).

Needless to say, such a pipeline would take years to be completed at best. And that assumes Gulf countries won’t oppose it politically and that the regime in Iran won’t be targeting it in future conflicts.

Iran's Hormuz “Toll Booth”: Another Long Term Question Mark

I think the escalation in Iran has exposed an uncomfortable reality: the fact that Iran can, even when weakened, “police” commercial passage through the Strait of Hormuz.

Iran has already been charging fees to allow passage of tankers in the Strait. The regime may want to keep doing so after a peace deal. I find this outcome especially likely when considering President Donald Trump’s latest statement about European allies having to “go get (their) own oil” after the U.S. withdraws from the region.

Given how unlikely it is European countries (or any country, for that matter) would want to pursue a war in Iran to “get their oil” without America, the next logical option is to negotiate with Iran.

To be clear, I do not think passage fees per se are the problem here. A few million per tanker are unlikely to represent a significant headwind in the context of global energy trade. The problem is rather the new geopolitical situation of it all.

Would Iran allow Gulf countries to even look for long term alternatives to the Strait of Hormuz?

Will we risk an escalation leading to conflicts any time the region tries to grow independent from Iran?

Ultimately, the risk is that the world may have to cut increasingly worse commercial and political deals with Iran's regime. That's why I fundamentally distrust the current relief rally. I think the market will need more time to digest a new reality that, today, still remains unclear.

What I Did During Times Of Market Fear: Buying Tech

One silver lining of the current situation in Iran (purely from an investing standpoint, of course) is that the U.S. Tech sector has continued its correction. Tech companies are now registering their cheapest premium against the broader market since 2019, as shown below.

That’s the main reason why I have added to my position in the Nasdaq-100 during this correction, which I regularly buy via the Invesco NASDAQ 100 ETF (QQQM), a cheaper and more efficient alternative to the Invesco QQQ Trust, Series 1 ETF (QQQ).

I have also increased my exposure to the triple leveraged ProShares UltraPro QQQ ETF (TQQQ), which is in line with my investing strategy for this year.

Is Tech The New Defensive Sector Today?

If we are just at the start of a prolonged correction and a U.S./Global recession, I think Tech stocks will outperform the broader market.

My belief is rooted in simple logic: traditionally “defensive” stocks, such as those in consumer goods or industrials, are going to be impacted far more than Tech stocks in case of a recession. While Tech stocks rely (for the most part) on more resilient B2B sales, consumer and industrial are directly exposed to consumer sentiment and investments.

Of course, I would still expect a recession to impact Tech stocks in the short term, and especially those tied to the advertising industry, such as Alphabet, Inc. (GOOGL) or Meta Platforms, Inc. (META). But ultimately, the AI race (and its CapEx) is long term unaffected by the situation in Iran.

If, as a company, you are in a race to automate your workforce and increase your margins by an order of magnitude over your competitors, you are going to do it regardless if you can borrow money at 3% or 6% due to the macro situation.

Furthermore, even if the AI race slows down, these companies were among the most profitable companies in the world, with the largest moats, well before the Iran war and the rise of AI.

In this regard, I see Tech stocks as the defensive sector today. They are relatively cheap and no less innovative or profitable than what they were before the war in Iran. Ultimately, as a long term AI bull, I find that any discount is a bliss.

Risks (And Reasons To Be More Optimistic)

The entire investment thesis in Tech today (including the so heavily discussed CapEx investment) is still tied to the development of AI. I covered my thoughts on Artificial General Intelligence in detail in my previous coverage and most recently with an article about Nvidia Corporation (NVDA). Readers that disagree with me and are skeptical about AI may want to stay out of Tech, even in the current context.

In regard to the war in Iran, its direction is impossible to predict as a geopolitical event. My hope is, of course, that the situation quickly resolves with minimal disruptions. This said, as I cannot predict geopolitics, I choose to simply add to my portfolio on weakness and avoid selling. Readers that believe they can properly time geopolitical events may benefit from doing so and staying outside equities for the time being.

Within the context of the Iran war, it is worth noting that there is also some reason to be optimistic. The current regime in Iran, which seems to still be largely in power, has historically been open to dealing with foreign powers, including a landmark nuclear deal with the US itself back in 2015. In this sense, the recent declarations from Iran’s president can indeed be seen as a sign of relief.

Conclusion

Any sign of de-escalation for the Iran war is a welcome development. This said, I expect disruption to global energy supply to last even after a ceasefire or a peace deal. This disruption cannot be understated, as it can be quantified as almost twice as large as that of the 1973 oil crisis overall.

With lots of moving pieces, including what a post-war Iran may mean for trade in the region, I find it difficult to be bullish today.

This said, I still recommend a Buy for Tech stocks, which are currently at their cheapest premium against the broader market since 2019. These stocks have always been among the most profitable and high-growth on the planet, and they benefit from large moats and leadership in the AI race. Personally, I think the AI race, net of minor disruption relative to helium and energy prices, is going nowhere long term.

As I find myself unable to time the market in times of geopolitical uncertainty, my next best strategy is to buy the Tech-heavy Nasdaq-100 index during times of correction. I plan to keep adding if we are to see the correction extending in a bear market.

data.title: J.Jill Is Now Undervalued After The Recent Sell-Off
data.description:
It sounds like some investors were waiting for J.Jill, Inc. (JILL) to crash after its fourth-quarter report, and now that’s happened. This clothing store’s management warned investors that it was going to post weak results, and now it’s done that. So, this stock now offers a potential buying opportunity for value investors. However, to avoid becoming a value trap, J.Jill will need to invest in marketing to appeal to younger customers. Like a few other stores I’ve written about, such as Bath & Body Works (BBWI), J.Jill has loyal older customers but might struggle to attract younger ones. But it might not gain these new customers by sending out more catalogs.

In Q4 2025, J.Jill reported that its revenue fell 3.1% to $138.4 million. This was still a beat because analysts’ expectations were already low. The store’s gross margin also fell 320 basis points to 63.1%. J.Jill is facing higher costs because of tariffs. Meanwhile, its core customers, older women, are also trading down to discount stores. So, this company is in a tough situation right now, and it’s not expected to get a lot better in 2026.

J.Jill is trading at very low multiples right now. It currently has a non-GAAP forward P/E of 6.42. Investors see it as a declining business. But I’m not sure if that’s true. It doesn’t sound like this company is closing underperforming stores to reduce its costs. Instead, it’s reopening stores in neighborhoods where wealthy women used to buy its clothing in the past. And there are reasons to believe that these new stores could perform well.

J.Jill's Core Customer Base Is Professional Women

I’ve always thought of J.Jill has a catalog store, but this brand does have 250 brick-and-mortar stores. Its core customers are now Gen X professional women. And J.Jill has very high gross margins. So, I’d expect to see its stores in upscale shopping malls where a lot of professionals live. It’s not surprising that the closest location in my area is next to the Whole Foods store in Santa Barbara. Its customers would probably also shop at high-end department stores like Saks and Nordstrom. And its customers would also be likely to read the investment articles on this site.

And I’d say that the biggest threat to a store like J.Jill is probably at discount stores such as T.J. Maxx (TJX). T.J. Maxx can sell professional clothing for much lower prices. Another challenge would be Urban Outfitters (URBN), both because of Anthropologie and because of its Nuuly subsidiary. In the past, women who shopped at stores like this wouldn’t use a clothing rental service, but they might do it now to save money.

J.Jill Is Still Adding New Stores

It seems like many investors think of J.Jill has a shrinking business with an older customer base, and that’s why it’s trading at low multiples. But this company is still growing its brick-and-mortar footprint. J.Jill added 4 net new stores in 2025 and is planning to add another 5 stores in 2026. So, I’d estimate that it will gain about a 2% uplift from new stores this year. That’s important because it’s guiding for -1% to -3% comps. If J.Jill reports -2% comps at the midpoint, but its location count rises by 2% as well; those factors will cancel each other out.

Adding new locations will also make this store more visible to younger consumers who probably weren’t getting J.Jill’s catalogs are in the mail. It’s like when other DTC stores, such as Warby Parker (WRBY), add brick-and-mortar stores so they’re not only relying on online marketing. Shoppers who weren’t familiar with this company before might see it next to a store like Anthropologie and realize that it’s another high-end store where they can buy quality clothing. Its core customers will pay high prices for clothing made with durable materials. They don’t want to buy a cheap dress from a discount store that will fall apart after they wash it a few times.

Many Of The New Stores Are Reentry Stores

On the Q4 2025 call, CFO Mark Webb said that many of J.Jill’s new stores are located in reentry markets. The company is opening new stores in locations where it previously closed stores. It expects these stores to ramp up quickly because those stores historically produced good results. That got my attention. If J.Jill’s stores were performing well; the company must have had a good reason to close them anyway. So, I decided to look up old earnings call transcripts to find out why the stores were closed in the first place.

It turns out that the stores were closed because of temporary headwinds. In Q4 2020, former CEO Claire Spofford explained that J.Jill closed many of its stores during the pandemic. The company wasn’t sure when its customers would come back, so it was trying to reduce its operating costs. During the pandemic, it wasn’t clear when the malls would reopen. Now that the pandemic is over, it makes sense for J.Jill to open stores in the same locations again.

Tariffs May Also Create Short-Term Headwinds

J.Jill does expect to report lower margins in 2026 because of tariffs. It might not have enough pricing power right now to pass the tariffs onto its customers. But J.Jill’s management also expects conditions to improve later in the year, and I still think that’s possible. If this store can maintain its product quality, its pricing power should come back. I’ve mentioned the K-shaped economy in other articles. This store has wealthier customers than other clothing stores, which is why it has high margins in the first place. And its competitors will also incur higher costs because of tariffs. So, while it looks like Q1 2026 could be tough, J.Jill may be able to raise its prices and pass on some tariff costs later in 2026.

Maintaining Product Quality Is The Biggest Risk Factor

I think the biggest risk factor for J.Jill is that management might be tempted to reduce costs by using lower-quality materials to make its clothing. This isn’t a fast-fashion store. It’s selling clothing to wealthy, mature women for relatively high prices. Its customers are going to notice if it starts using lower-cost materials. There is a real risk that some customers may trade down to lower-cost stores to save money in the short term, but they’re a lot more likely to come back later if J.Jill maintains its product quality. It doesn’t sound like the company plans to do this, but I still think this is a risk factor.

Comparative Valuation For J.Jill

J.Jill is still trading at a discount in comparison to other clothing stores. Again, on March 31, it had a non-GAAP forward P/E of 6.42. On the same date, Urban Outfitters had a non-GAAP forward P/E of 10.39. And The GAP (GAP) had a forward non-GAAP P/E of 10.36. The other clothing stores are also being impacted by tariffs, so they’re facing the same pressure on their margins. But I think J.Jill is trading at a discount because investors also expect its revenue to shrink over the long term.

My J.Jill Valuation For 2026

Again, I think J.Jill will probably report -2% comps in 2026 based on its guidance, but its new stores will make up for that. Because it’s opening up reentry stores, they’ll ramp up faster than stores in locations where it hasn’t operated before. So, I’d say flat revenue growth is possible, even though J.Jill guided for 0% to -2% revenue growth in 2026. In 2025, J.Jill posted revenue of $596.5 million with an 8.5% operating margin.

I’ll use two scenarios here to explain why J.Jill looks undervalued. J.Jill guided for $70-$75 million in adjusted EBITDA in 2026. That’s $72.5 million at the midpoint. So, if I subtract $21.3 million in depreciation, its adjusted EBIT would be $51.2 million. With a 25% tax rate, it would report $38.4 million in net income. This company has 15.3 million diluted shares outstanding. So, its adjusted EPS would be $2.51 per share. With a forward P/E of 6, a one-year price target based on its current guidance would be $15.06.

But I could also estimate J.Jill’s 2026 GAAP results are mine instead of using the company’s adjusted figures. If its operating margin fell 50 basis points to 8.0% because of tariffs, and its revenue growth was flat, its 2026 operating income would be $47.7 million. It also looks like J.Jill will report about $2 million in interest expenses, so if I subtract that, I get $45.7 million in EBIT. With a 25% tax rate, its net income would be $34.3 million. That would result in full-year EPS of $2.24. With a 2026 P/E of 6, my one-year target price would be $13.44. J.Jill’s stock price after the close on March 31, 2026, was $11.46, so my price target is still 17% higher than the current price.

Wall Street analysts currently expect J.Jill to report $1.96 in EPS in 2026. But the company’s current guidance implies that its adjusted EPS will be a lot higher. And if I make my own projection with the tariff impact included, I also get a higher EPS figure.

Conclusion

I’d say J.Jill is undervalued. Investors might be placing too much emphasis on the store’s weak Q1 guidance instead of its full-year 2026 guidance. This store is still adding new locations, so even though its comps are currently negative, its revenue growth may return in the future. And even if its margins weaken because of tariffs, it still looks very likely to report higher EPS in 2026 than analysts currently expect. Value investors may have gotten the opportunity they were waiting for here. So, I’d say J.Jill is a Buy.

data.title: Upstart: Buy The Meltdown - Compellingly Valued AI Lender With Profitable Growth
data.description:
I previously covered Upstart Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ:UPST) in January 2026, discussing why I had reiterated my Buy rating then, thanks to the materialization of a much needed correction from the prior highs, the consequently cheaper valuations, and the excellent insights offered by the established trading pattern.

This was on top of the fintech's notably improved operating leverage from the prior lack of profitability, the excellent monetization of its AI lending platform, and the robust tailwinds arising from the likely to be lower borrowing cost environment in 2026.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am cautiously reiterating my Buy rating for the UPST stock, albeit preferably nearer to the 2023/2024 trading floor of $20s for an improved margin of safety.

My optimism is attributed to the improved risk/reward from the uncertain macroeconomic environment/ongoing selloff, their profitable growth prospects as observed in the 2025-2028 targets, and their oversold technical indicators.

This is despite the intermediate term risks from the uncertain macroeconomic/geopolitical environment and the elevated short interest ratio.

UPST Faces Numerous Headwinds & Tailwinds

Since my last Hold rating, the bears have proven my bullish thesis wrong, as UPST lost more than half of its value while breaching its uptrend support line established since May 2023, with a similar correction also observed in its fintech peers in varying degrees.

Part of their headwinds may be attributed to the uncertain macroeconomic/geopolitical uncertainties and the likely to be higher interest rate environment, with it likely to trigger headwinds to the fintech's loan origination business.

As a result of the potentially impacted prospects, I can understand why the market has decided to de-risk first and ask questions later, as observed in UPST's steep meltdown of -71.6% from the 52-week high along with the Global X FinTech ETF (FINX) -37.4% selloff from the 52-week high.

1. Proven AI Lender Platform

For now, I am of the opinion that UPST's selloff has been unwarranted, given their excellent FQ4 2025 metrics and the promising FY2026/2025-2028 guidance, thanks to the higher loan originations from the prior Fed rate cuts in late 2025.

For example, UPST already reports:

  • the higher Transaction Volumes of 455.78K loans in FQ4 2025 (+86% YoY) and
  • the total loan originations of $3.2B (+52% YoY),

with the higher adoption of their platform already driving:

  • the expanding conversion rate at 19.4% (+1.4 points YoY/+4.5 from FQ4 2019 levels of 14.9%) and
  • the platform/referral fee revenue growth to $265.22M (+33% YoY).

These underscore the success of UPST's AI-powered lending marketplace, especially since fee revenues continue to comprise a large part of its top lines at a ratio of 89.5% in FQ4 2025 (-1.5 points YoY), with the lower reliance on net interest incomes implying their long-term resilience beyond the fluctuating interest rate environment.

This is significantly aided by the lower loans held on the balance sheet by -20% QoQ, with it underscoring their excellent risk management at a time of uncertain macroeconomic environment, as they hint at further moderation moving forward thanks to their growing funding partners entering 2026.

2. Intermediate-Term Macro Risks

On the other hand, readers cannot deny the impact of the likely to be higher borrowing cost environment on UPST's loan origination/refinancing prospects, as some (albeit a small group of) analysts already price in a potential Fed rate hike in 2026.

This is especially since their FQ4 2025 tailwinds are mostly attributed to the previously robust auto loan originations at +344% YoY and home loan originations at +355% YoY, with it remaining to be seen if the same may occur in 2026.

My caution is attributed to:

  • The lower total vehicle sales on a YTD basis by -3.2% YoY, attributed to the "auto consumers continue to face affordability issues" and
  • The "number of home buyers in the market falls to a historic low" as of February 2026, attributed to the "stubbornly high home prices and mortgage rates, layoffs, and mounting economic and political uncertainty."

Given that further Fed rate cuts are seemingly "off the table," I urge readers to closely monitor UPST's future performance metrics against their ambitious 2025-2028 growth targets, since any performance miss is likely to bring forth painful stock price corrections.

3. Compellingly Valued, Profitable Growth Prospects

For now, UPST has further highlighted their profitable growth prospects through:

  • The promising FY2026 revenue guidance of $1.4B (+40% YoY) and adj. EBITDA margin guidance of 21% (-1 point YoY),
  • While offering an aggressive revenue growth guidance at a 3Y CAGR of approximately +35% and FY2028 adj. EBITDA margins of approximately 25%,

with it building upon the FY2025 outperformance at +64% YoY and 22% (+20 points YoY), respectively.

As a result, I can understand why the consensus has already raised their forward estimates, with UPST expected to report an accelerated top/bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +30.4%/+27.4% through FY2028, compared to the 5Y historical top-line growth of a CAGR of +34.9% and their reversal to bottom-line profitability in FY2025.

As a result of the -71.6% meltdown from the 52 week highs and the raised consensus forward estimates, it goes without saying that UPST is now even more compelling at FWD EV/EBITDA valuations of 12.13x and FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 11.29x, down from the recent highs of 42.12x/51.05x while nearing the sector median of 8.69x/9.80x, respectively.

This reason is also why the stock is attractively valued at my estimated 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 0.41x, based on the FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 11.29x and the consensus adj EPS growth estimates at a 3Y CAGR of +27.4%, compared to the sector median of 0.91x.

When comparing UPST's FWD EV/EBITDA valuations of 12.13x, the FY2025 adj. EBITDA margins of 22%, and my estimated 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 0.41x to its fintech peers, including:

  • PSP/P2P/BNPL - Block, Inc. (XYZ) at 8.03x/14.3% in FY2025/0.44x,
  • BNPL - Affirm Holdings, Inc. (AFRM) at 19.25x/27% over the LTM/0.37x,
  • neo bank/investment - SoFi Technologies, Inc. (SOFI) at NA/29% in FY2025/0.66x, and
  • PSP/P2P/BNPL - PayPal Holdings, Inc. (PYPL) at 6.53x/22.1% in FY2025/1.26x, respectively,

it is apparent that UPST's profitable growth prospects are still compellingly valued here, aside from neo bank - LendingClub Corporation (LC) at 1.49x/24% in FY2025/0.18x.

Otherwise, readers may want to pay close attention to UPST's balance sheet health, as observed in the growing net debt of $1.17B in FY2025 (-90.5% YoY) and the still elevated net debt to adj EBITDA ratio of 5.08x (compared to a year ago at 57.92x) against XYZ at -0.06x (given the higher cash than debt), AFRM at -1.08x, and PYPL at -0.05x.

Combined with the likely prolonged macroeconomic normalization cadence, as the Fed expects the core PCE inflation to moderate to 2% only by 2028, I am of the opinion that the stock is likely to remain volatile in the meantime, worsened by the still elevated short interest ratio of 29.27% by the time of writing.

UPST Is An Even Better Buy After The Selloff

UPST has already breached the uptrend support line established since the May 2023 bottom, with the stock yet to find a floor despite the tapering downward momentum since late February 2026.

For now, I am of the opinion that the selloff has been a boon indeed, given that the stock is already trading near to my base-case fair value estimates, based on the calculation as below:

  • The FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 11.29x (cheap against the peer group mean of ~15x),

  • My estimated FY2025 adj. EPS of $1.93 (+3,960% YoY, based on the FY2025 net incomes of $53.60M, FY2025 SBC expenses of $131.95M, and the FY2025 shares outstanding of 96.03M),

  • The multiplication of the FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 11.29x and my estimated FY2025 adj EPS of $1.93, and

  • The consequent base-case fair value estimates of $21.70.

Based on the consensus FY2028 adj EPS estimates of $3.90, there remains an excellent upside potential of +77.6% to my base-case long-term price target of $44.00 as well.

This is not even taking into account the potential upgrading in UPST's valuations nearer to the peer group P/E mean of ~15x (still cheap compared to UPST's 1Y P/E mean of ~27x), with it triggering the more than doubling upside potential to my bull-case long-term price target of $58.50.

The management has also signaled their focus on shareholder returns, as they seemingly seek to offset the ongoing equity dilution from their Stock-Based Compensation through share repurchases.

As a result of the improved risk/reward from the uncertain macroeconomic environment/ongoing selloff, their profitable growth prospects as observed in the 2025-2028 targets, and their oversold technical indicators, I am cautiously reiterating my Buy rating for UPST.

Caveats To Buy Rating

Given UPST's mixed technical indicators across the oversold RSIs and trading volumes, with the stock seemingly on its way to retest the November 2023 low of $20s in the near-term despite already losing -71.6% of its value since the 52-week high, it goes without saying that the stock is only suitable for those with a higher risk appetite and a longer investing trajectory.

This is because it remains to be seen when the macroeconomic environment and the market sentiments may improve, with it uncertain when a trading floor may materialize.

As a result, my reiterated Buy rating comes with the caveat that investors observe the stock price movement for a little longer and only add nearer to the 2023/2024 trading floor of $20s for an improved margin of safety.

data.title: One Thing Missing From Yesterday's Rally
data.description:
We ended the month with a tremendous war-torn rally in stocks, as the Dow Jones Industrials soared more than 1,000 points and the S&P 500 roared back 3%, led by an oversold technology sector. It wasn’t President Trump’s repeated assurances that lifted risk asset prices as much as a report that followed, indicating Iran is ready to end the war provided certain guarantees are met. President Trump has apparently given up on the goal of forcibly reopening the Strait of Hormuz, instead leaving it up to the countries that use it to work things out with Iran. He confirmed after the market closed that military operations would likely end in the next two to three weeks.

From the start, my forecast was that the President would pivot within a month of the start of the war on the premise that he would fold under the pressure of rising fuel costs, deteriorating poll numbers, and the risk of a bear market and prolonged economic downturn in advance of the midterm elections. I believed markets would dictate policy, and it is clearly evident that they did yesterday. Yet while stock prices soared and bond yields eased, the missing link to yesterday’s monster rally was a commensurate drop in oil prices. That will limit the extent of the upside until we see a meaningful decline.

WTI Crude did come off its high of $106.46 in the morning hours, but $102 and change is hardly anything to celebrate. Oil has not pulled back because the Trump administration has no definitive plans to reopen the strait, as the President assumes that once we leave the region, the waterway will open “automatically.” I doubt that, unless concessions are made to Iran, which is a question mark. International pressure from European countries that did not participate in the attack should have some influence, but until the strait is opened, we are likely to see oil prices remain elevated.

President Trump’s pivot shows that he is very sensitive to both stock prices and the surge in gasoline, which he says will drop in price abruptly and dramatically when the conflict ends. With the national average now over $4, pressure will mount to exit rapidly if he wants to see that happen. I suspect he will reiterate this assertion in his speech this evening at 9pm. The problem is that gas prices rise alongside oil prices immediately, but they take much longer to subside as oil prices come down. It will weigh on consumer spending growth in the months ahead.

Surprisingly, consumer confidence inched higher for the month of March in yesterday’s Conference Board survey due to a slight improvement in present conditions. Higher fuel costs led to a jump in inflation expectations, which weighed on future expectations. At the same time, prior concerns about trade and tariffs waned noticeably. It seems consumers have traded one concern for another rather than having one stacked on top of another, which is encouraging. The outlook for the labor market remained unchanged.

The Job Openings and Labor Turnover Survey (JOLTS) indicates that job openings fell in February, while hiring continued to slow in a labor market that continues to be characterized by limited hiring and limited firings. Again, it is encouraging that we did not see further deterioration, but the month of war in the Middle East isn’t likely to improve these numbers at all.

I think this rally has legs up to the point when a ceasefire is declared and an official de-escalation is in motion. At the same time, I will be assessing the extent of the damage imposed on the domestic economy, as well as the global economy, from which S&P 500 companies derive some 40% of their revenues, to determine whether this rally is an opportunity to lighten up on risk assets for a retest of recent lows or not.

The good news is that the rolling bear market in stocks has meaningfully lowered valuations. The drawdown in the Nasdaq Composite from its all-time high was approximately 13%, but investors should be aware that the average stock has already declined some 33% since this correction began. By the same token, the drawdown of approximately 9% in the S&P 500 saw the average stock fall 18%. Diversified portfolios fared much better because exposure to the energy, materials, utilities, and consumer defensive sectors led to gains this year. Meanwhile, financials, consumer cyclicals, and technology sectors suffered the brunt of the decline. And if you were a good stock picker, there was plenty to celebrate, as many names bucked the decline altogether. It is truly a stock picker's market.

data.title: Strategy: Down 77% And Still Isn't Cheap Enough
data.description:
The last time I wrote about Strategy Inc (MSTR) stock was in November 2024, the day when MSTR stock topped. My article was titled "MicroStrategy Stock: Signs Of Topping Have Appeared." I argued that the technical setup for both MSTR and Bitcoin (BTC-USD) wasn't great and that cracks were forming in the sentiment around MSTR stock, which was way too hot at the time.

Lots of people didn't like my article because I went against the bulls. See below.

Now, with MSTR stock 77% off its highs and BTC 46% off its highs, the story is a lot different. In other words, I don't want to poke my eyes out when looking at Strategy anymore (at least not as much). The reason is that the premium to mNAV, when using enterprise value, is now at 1.14x, a major improvement compared to the 3x+ multiples the stock used to carry. And its market cap is actually under its BTC holdings.

Bitcoin's chart is also around a major support level, which can help stabilize MSTR stock for now. And the Crypto Fear & Greed Index is in fear territory. That's generally viewed as a good contrarian signal.

Based on what I said above, you might think I've flipped to being bullish, but I give it a Hold rating because there are factors that offset the positives. Let's get into it.

Why MSTR Isn't A Buy

Basically, I don't think there's a clear Buy signal just yet. There's still a 14% premium to mNAV on an enterprise value basis, and that can come down to 1x if Bitcoin ends up making one more swing lower. I really don't see any reason to pay a premium for MSTR at the moment.

Not to mention, Bitcoin and BTC-related stocks are more like sentiment plays than fundamental ones because there aren't traditional cash flows to value them on. You need good reasons to believe that sentiment will improve.

At the current price, with its market cap under its BTC holdings, it's harder for MSTR to raise money in an accretive way. That's a possible reason why Strategy didn't buy any Bitcoin last week.

This can hurt its performance, especially if its mNAV multiple stays at around this level for a while. That's because the company won't be able to quickly increase its BTC per share and because it's still on the hook for dividend and debt/interest payments (without generating any cash flow). The company will basically bleed cash on its payments if things stay the way they are.

Since I'm more of a traditional investor who tends to buy stocks with real cash flows, I'd only ever consider MSTR stock in very particular scenarios. Those scenarios are 1) a true BTC panic where sentiment is absurdly low and the stock trades at or below 1.0x mNAV (using enterprise value) or 2) when the stock is clearly trending higher and I'm just trying to take advantage of short-term momentum.

Right now, we're in a bit of a wait-and-see phase. Plus, if the Middle East conflict lasts longer than expected, BTC isn't likely to be a safe haven, and that can hurt MSTR. Therefore, I give it a Hold rating. It's been derisked a lot, which is why I don't want to give it a Sell rating, but it's just not "obvious."

The Premium Still Has Room To Come Down

If you look at Strategy's mNAV chart on a website like strategytracker.com, you'll see that it shows a 0.91x mNAV.

That's true if you ignore debt, but the actual mNAV, according to Strategy itself, is 1.14x.

Now, let's be fair. The mNAV chart above shows that we're at very low levels. The mNAV multiple was around 4x in November 2024, and even near its low points in 2023, it was around 1x. So lots of the speculative excess is gone.

But the final thing I'd want to see to fully conclude that speculative excess is gone is for the enterprise value mNAV multiple to drop to 1x or lower. Then, we can truly say there's absolutely no premium being assigned to MSTR.

1x has the potential to act as a support level for the stock, assuming stable Bitcoin prices. On the other hand, it's also worth noting that it doesn't have to act as a support. For example, Sharplink (SBET), an Ethereum (ETH-USD) treasury company with no debt or preferred shares, actually trades at 0.75x its fully diluted mNAV. Does that make sense fundamentally? I don't think so. But it goes to show how much sentiment can affect pricing.

My point is that waiting for a 1x multiple at least ensures you won't overpay relative to its holdings and net debt position.

Strategy Bleeds Cash While Waiting For A Rebound

Strategy has four series of preferred shares that it issued over the years. The most popular is the Strategy Inc 9.0% SERIES A PERPETUAL STRETCH PREF STK (STRC) preferred shares, with a market cap just over $5 billion and an 11.5% yield. Because of its heavy issuance of preferred shares, it now has to pay $1.083 billion in dividends every year. With its current cash reserve of $2.25 billion, that's just 24.9 months of dividend coverage.

It also has to make around $34.6 million in interest payments over the course of a year based on an average coupon of 0.421% and a notional debt value of $8.214 billion.

That's over $1.1 billion per year in payments, while its TTM free cash flow sits at -$112.5 million. If BTC stays flat or falls, MSTR will have trouble raising money at favorable rates to make these dividend payments. It'll just be depleting its cash position, making money for preferred shareholders, not for common shareholders.

The longer BTC stays depressed, the worse it is for MSTR.

Middle East Tensions Add To The Uncertainty

While I'm generally an optimist and hope/believe that conflicts will de-escalate quickly, the truth is, we don't really know how long things will take to play out. CBS News reported that the war can end in 2-3 weeks, according to Trump. If it lasts longer than expected, oil prices can stay high, which can cause inflation and kill any rate cut hopes.

That's a negative for Bitcoin and risk assets. Many people see BTC as an inflation hedge, but when inflation was high in 2022, Bitcoin tanked, and so did MSTR. Let's be real. Bitcoin moves higher when sentiment is hot, not when inflation worries are back on the table. In the longer term, it's likely that this war will be a blip on the radar, but it's still worth considering as a short-term risk. It can make sense to wait for more clarity on the situation.

Now, let's look at some of the positives.

Bitcoin Chart Is At Support

Bitcoin obviously moves in waves. You can see that in the chart below.

We're in a big downswing that's now sitting near past levels of resistance (previous resistance is potentially becoming support). We're also near the 200-week simple moving average, which has been a good area to buy in the past. If history repeats itself, the next move will be higher eventually.

Even the Fear & Greed Index of 30 out of 100, which signals fear, is a good contrarian signal. But it's also not a guaranteed signal, and I'd rather it be in extreme fear territory if I'm looking to be a contrarian.

Overall, the technical setup is positive for MSTR bulls, but given everything I said above, I'd rather wait for more confirmation of a bottom in BTC or an outright panic that leads to better prices.

The Bottom Line On MSTR Stock

MSTR stock is much safer now than it was when I last wrote about it in November 2024. Back then, you would have been smart to sell. Now, the move is more nuanced. It's not an obvious Buy because there's no catalyst at the moment to send shares higher (meaning MSTR will bleed cash while it waits for a rebound), and it's not an obvious sell since the valuation has come down so much. It's a wait-and-see play.

That's the move now. Do absolutely nothing and be patient. MSTR is a Hold.

data.title: Schrödinger: Transition Pressure And Competition Offset Ecosystem Strength
data.description:
Based in the US, Schrödinger (SDGR) is one of the leading software companies in the scientific and biotechnology verticals, developing a computational platform to accelerate drug discovery and materials science. It mainly makes money through software licensing and collaborative drug discovery programs with major pharmaceutical companies. Companies like Certara (CERT), Simulation Plus, and Recursion Pharmaceuticals (RXRX) are some of the competitors in this space.

Since its 2020 IPO, share performance has been underwhelming for the most part. SDGR plummeted from an all-time high of over $77 to recent lows of around $11, which is where it is more or less trading at. Sentiment has largely been negative, as SDGR is down almost -40% over the past year alone, driven mostly by the -36% decline in share prices YTD alone.

I initiated my coverage on SDGR with a neutral rating. My 1-year price target of $10.6 per share projects a -6% decline YoY. My analysis found that SDGR remains hard to fairly value due to the transition in its software business model, which affects its revenue growth, suggesting that it is more prudent to stay on the sidelines for now.

Financial Reviews

In my opinion, fundamentals are a bit choppy, mainly due to the major business model transition from on-premise to a hosted software delivery model. Based on my experience covering software stocks, many similar transitions could indeed present a near-term volatility since they may produce some difficulties in fairly valuing the business, as I will touch on later in the valuation section. On the flip side, though, I think it is done for the benefit of more robust and visible long-term growth.

In Q4 2025, for instance, underlying growth remains solid, in my view, despite the reported Q4 top-line numbers appearing weak on the software side. SDGR saw a -13% quarterly revenue decline YoY in the software business due to revenue recognition timing as a result of transition despite steady ACV ((Annual Contract Value)), as per the management’s comment in the 2026 KeyBanc Healthcare Market Forum:

And then we also announced a change to focus and emphasize hosted contracts in the software business as opposed to on-premise contracts. We think that will also take about 3 years to transition over. Today, it's about 25% hosted. We expect to get to 75% hosted. Given the way revenue recognition works, this is a very common transition for companies to make towards hosted and SaaS solutions. But in the near term, it will have the impact of reducing revenue, especially in 2026.

Source: 2026 KeyBanc Healthcare Market Forum.

For the most part, this has been the source of disparity not only between its underlying ACV growth and reported software revenue but also between its software and drug business. In Q4, revenue was $87.2 million, a -1.2% YoY decline. However, from an FY standpoint, SDGR delivered $255.9 million of revenue, a 23% YoY increase, driven by the doubling of drug discovery revenue.

Profitability appears better YoY in Q4, though adjusted EBITDA (aEBITDA) remains in the negatives despite the narrowing losses. From an FY standpoint, aEBITDA loss was around -$115 million, narrowing significantly from that of last year. One of the key positives as of Q4 is the -9% YoY decrease, which suggests a stronger cost discipline on the management's part.

As of FY 2025, liquidity remains robust, while operating cash flow (OCF) generation has relatively improved. SDGR had cash and marketable securities of over $400 million, a higher level than last year’s, with OCF of almost $14 million, a massive swing from last year’s -$157 million OCF loss.

Catalyst

In my opinion, there seems to be a moderate to strong catalyst going into 2026, which I think hinges on the continuation of the strength in the drug discovery business.

While SDGR could potentially see a re-rating as soon as it completes the transition into the hosted model, my view is that it will remain unlikely in FY 2026. Instead, though the 10% to 15% software ACV growth remains promising, I think that in 2026, market sentiment would still likely be driven by the performance in the drug discovery business segment, which is an undisrupted, proven revenue stream that presents a decent outlook.

To support that business, I think it is important to note that as of FY 2025, SDGR has a relatively complete ecosystem to make it happen. First, as a software developer, it has proven that its predictive computational model is working in real life by demonstrating strong growth in the drug discovery business and actively doing trials. Secondly, SDGR also has built a strong relationship with major pharmaceutical companies to accelerate commercialization of programs in its pipeline, as we saw in FY 2025, thanks to the company’s successful collaboration with Novartis.

Overall, the collaboration program pipeline is strong, in my opinion, suggesting that SDGR could potentially deliver its promise of $55 million to $65 million of drug discovery revenue in FY 2026 by simply tapping into its deep partnership network.

Risk

Risk remains moderate to strong as well, in my opinion, especially given the fact that AI-driven competitors continue to make drug discovery more crowded and competitive today. Furthermore, some of these competitors have also inked deals with SDGR’s existing partners, such as Eli Lilly (LLY), which just a few days ago signed a $2.75 billion deal with Insilico, another competitor, on AI-driven drug discovery, according to Reuters.

In my view, the AI-driven drug discovery market will also still evolve going forward, with certain business or computational models possibly being more preferred than the others, also posing a potential risk for SDGR. For instance, some of these competitors, like RXRX or recently funded Xaira, seem to employ different approaches than SDGR.

Valuation/Pricing

My target price for SDGR is driven by the following assumptions for the bull vs. bear scenarios of the FY 2026 target price model based on the consensus estimate:

  1. Bull scenario (50% probability) assumptions - I project SDGR to see successful trials, which again should attract commercialization potential with some of the pharma companies in its network, delivering drug discovery revenue near the $65 million target for the FY. Meanwhile, though I think that the hosted software transition may not be fully complete by the end of the FY, the progress is satisfactory and in line with the management’s expectation. In line with its forward P/S valuation assessment, I assign SDGR a 3.2x P/S.

  2. Bear scenario (50% probability) assumptions - I project SDGR to potentially see delays in its trials. In the midst of increasing competition as well as a transition that is affecting its revenue recognition, the market’s sentiment on the stock remains negative. I assign SDGR a P/S of 2x, lower than its TTM P/S today.

Consolidating all the information above into my model, I arrived at an FY 2026 weighted target price of $10.7 per share, projecting about a -6% decline YoY.

At this point, I would rate SDGR neutral. I think that while I lean more towards the bullish side on SDGR after learning more about its current progress and fundamentals, it just seems that due to the transition in its software business that depressed its forecasted revenue growth, fairly valuing the company becomes quite challenging. As such, I would note that it is probably better for investors to stay on the sidelines for now.

Conclusion

As of FY 2025, SDGR has shown decent fundamentals and a solid business development outlook across its software and drug discovery businesses. The consistent decline in software revenue growth as a result of the transition from on-premise to hosted software models may have been the reason as to why its share price has been seeing some pressure YTD. Nonetheless, another thing that could present a risk is the increased competition and trial uncertainty despite the strong business ecosystem SDGR has developed through its major pharmaceutical partnership network. In my opinion, these uncertainties are captured in the massive gap between the bull and bear case revenues that have resulted in a lower-quality price target forecast. As such, I will rate the stock neutral and remain on the sideline this time.

data.title: Sony Group: A Wide Moat Compounder At A 15x P/E Gift (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
It's been a few months since my Sony (SONY) article. My first coverage was right after fiscal Q2 2026, and in short, my opinion of the stock was that, although it is a potential compounder with wide moats, it was not the time to buy. There were still some uncertainties regarding the gaming market, but mainly, the valuation was not good enough.

Since then, SONY stock has declined 30%. So, now, the scenario seems completely different to me. The Valuation? Much better. There is already a margin of safety and asymmetry. But besides that, there are also important triggers for Sony in the coming quarters that can unlock a lot of value, and I think the market is not pricing this correctly, which makes the case even more appealing.

For this reason, I am upgrading my rating on Sony to a buy.

Sony: A Word About Recent Results and Moats

Recapping the case a bit, the reason I like Sony a lot for the long term is its diversification into businesses with wide moats. Here, we truly have that meaning of a large Japanese conglomerate that was very famous a few decades ago. There is even a term for this, which I don't think is very famous today: Keiretsu, which focuses on various shareholdings simultaneously with a collaborative long-term focus.

Sony is not a video game company. I also don't think it can be said to be exactly an entertainment company, although the majority of revenue today is related to the video game, movies, or music industry. Because besides these three, Sony is still a global leader in camera sensors (used in smartphones) and so on.

The table below shows well how the revenue is diversified. We have strong revenue in games & network services, which comes from streams like hardware sales and services related to the PlayStation 5. But it is also possible to notice how Imaging & Sensing (the sensors mentioned earlier) is quite strong and has a high operating margin, which makes it still relevant in operating income (about 22%).

The Music division is also very interesting. Music royalties are a very good business. The reason is mainly the characteristic of resilience. Royalties from famous songs recorded 10, or 20 years ago continue to generate revenue to this day; it is a very rich asset. Movies released in 2026, in fact, movies that will still be released in 2030, could very well use soundtracks from artists of previous decades. Likewise, people will continue to use music streaming (read: Spotify (SPOT), YouTube Music, and similar) to listen to songs that Sony Group owns for years and years, generating more revenue at a low cost, almost as if it were an asset that generates "passive" income in an almost perpetual way.

Proving this point, the top-line CAGR was almost 15% in the last 4 years, and the operating income CAGR was 18% in that same period.

Even though it is a resilient business, it is not a boring business. There is a lot happening in the industry; recently Sony Music bought the Queen catalog, for instance. This means that with every play someone gives to Bohemian Rhapsody on Spotify, a small revenue is generated for Sony.

Returning to the rest of the operations, even if Music revenue is smaller than Games, Entertainment and Imaging, its operating income is larger than all of these, being responsible for almost a third of the operating income from continued operations.

And this was something I mentioned in my last article. Even though it is very diverse, the businesses end up complementing each other. A cinema IP can be used in games, and then there are the intangible benefits like increasing the notoriety of an IP in cinema to generate demand for games, but also tangible benefits like decreasing costs with music royalties, and verticalization of the operation (producing the hardware, which is the PS5 console, but also producing the services, producing exclusive games, etc.).

Moving to the most recent results, fiscal Q3 was not very good news; revenue remained flat YoY, but I liked that operating income increased 22%, driven not only by an optimization of the "corporate and elimination" part but also by the better margin of the Imaging & Sensing segment. If you go back to the first image of the article, you will also see that in Feb., there were positive revisions for the FY, with better sales expectations driven by games, and also better operating income driven by music and Imaging & Sensing solutions.

In short, what I want to say here is that Sony continues to be an outstanding company. Its business is "spot-on," with solid moats, and solid prospects for games, music, and similar, and all of this makes it possible to envision a compounding in the long term.

The GTA VI Trigger

Besides all these positive aspects in the long term, there is a very interesting trigger for the end of the calendar year 2026: GTA VI.

I did a very complete analysis of Take-Two Interactive (TTWO), and if you want to understand the trigger from Take-Two's point of view in more detail, I suggest you read it here. But summarizing the trigger, GTA VI is probably the most anticipated product in the entertainment industry ever. Expectations are for sales of millions of units in the first days of release, and dozens of millions of units throughout the first year.

This in itself already benefits Sony. With an installed base of ~90 million PS5s around the world, if it sells ~30 million units of a third-party game, it means literally hundreds of millions of dollars just from the platform fee (take rate).

But I think the benefit for Sony goes beyond software sales. I believe there will be a strong push for PS5 and PS5 Pro sales because of GTA VI. Many people who do not own a console will want to play. And this comes at a moment when components are more expensive (like SSDs and memory), and Sony just did a price hike on PS5s (check it in this news). I don't think a $100 price increase will decrease demand, due to the GTA VI trigger.

Now the PS5 Pro, which is a version that should handle GTA VI in a more "optimized" way, is costing $899 in the US, while the PS5 digital edition is costing $599, and the PS5 "disk edition" is at $649. Let's assume that GTA VI is a catalyst for selling 1 million PS5 Pros and 2.5 million PS5 digital editions. That would be $2.4 billion in revenue. Estimated revenue for Sony's FY is ~$28 billion (¥4.6 trillion). That is, for the next fiscal year, we are talking about a growth of more than 8% just with console sales, and that is in reasonable estimates. The current scenario could very well trigger sales of 5 million PS5s in total, not to mention if there is any special edition, or some combo with the PS6 in the coming ones, which would generate even more value.

Another point is that this increases the installed base, so if the player bought the PS5 to play GTA VI, they will probably subscribe to PlayStation products, and buy another PlayStation game during the year, and this increases revenue structurally.

Risks, Valuation and Bottom Line

This does not mean that there are no risks for Sony. Even with these tailwinds, we are still going through a complex scenario that ends up creating some headwinds. Production cost is one of them. I imagine that console sales in 2026 will be good enough to create an operating leverage. But if costs continue rising, the hardware margin continues to be pressured, even with these trends. This could reach a level where either Sony absorbs the costs significantly or increases the price (even more), which could generate a negative reaction. Besides that, we also have execution risk, such as game production (which costs a lot) that may not give the desired result, like some live services that were failures, such as Concord.

Furthermore, as it is a verticalized company and operates in console hardware but also in Imaging & Sensing, there are also impacts from geopolitical trends beyond the supply chain, such as tariffs, which affect demand for its products and the margin. Besides that, being a global company, FX also affects a lot. So if there is a massive devaluation in the JPY, this affects the consolidated revenue (when we look in USD).

But even if these risks have become a bit more evident in the last quarters, I think SONY stock has become more attractive. Besides the triggers and the solidity, the valuation has become much better. The drop of almost 30% in the stock in recent months has brought the forward P/E to 15x.

15x earnings for a compounder with clear triggers, which on average grew EBIT by ~12% in the last 10 years, is very cheap. For comparison purposes, the avg. of the last 5y of Sony's fwd P/E was 18x, i.e., there is a 13% discount relative to this average.

That's why I think that now, a buy rating is worth it for Sony stock.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Service Properties Trust Is Navigating Their Debt Problem
data.description:
